CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from problem motivation through methodological description (network construction, embedding enrichment, topological features) to experiments and conclusions; main contributions and empirical claims are stated early. Notation and experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters reported) appear sufficiently specified to aid comprehension, and writing quality is described as good with thorough related work coverage. Minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., code availability not mentioned and the summary does not confirm the presence/effectiveness of figures/tables or explicit illustrative examples), so while overall clarity is strong, a few presentation details could be improved. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and communicates its approach clearly: the pipeline (network construction, embedding enrichment, topological features, classifiers and ensemble/multi-view combinations) and datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, Arizona) are described in logical sequence. The main contributions are stated early and experiments report accuracy gains and hyperparameters, which supports reproducibility. Notation appears consistent and figures/tables are reportedly effective, and writing quality is good, though a few sections (e.g., discussion of limitations for very short/linear networks and the handling of parsing errors) could use slightly more concrete examples or clarification for clinical deployment. From a practitioner perspective the presentation favors practical details and cross-lingual evaluation, making it accessible with only minor presentation improvements needed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from problem motivation through methodological description (network construction, embedding enrichment, topological features) to experiments and conclusions; main contributions and empirical claims are stated early. Notation and experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters reported) appear sufficiently specified to aid comprehension, and writing quality is described as good with thorough related work coverage. Minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., code availability not mentioned and the summary does not confirm the presence/effectiveness of figures/tables or explicit illustrative examples), so while overall clarity is strong, a few presentation details could be improved.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and communicates its approach clearly: the pipeline (network construction, embedding enrichment, topological features, classifiers and ensemble/multi-view combinations) and datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, Arizona) are described in logical sequence. The main contributions are stated early and experiments report accuracy gains and hyperparameters, which supports reproducibility. Notation appears consistent and figures/tables are reportedly effective, and writing quality is good, though a few sections (e.g., discussion of limitations for very short/linear networks and the handling of parsing errors) could use slightly more concrete examples or clarification for clinical deployment. From a practitioner perspective the presentation favors practical details and cross-lingual evaluation, making it accessible with only minor presentation improvements needed.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper uses reasonable, domain-relevant baselines and evaluates on multiple datasets, and also that it explores contributions of topology, embeddings, and ensemble combinations. However, they both note the absence of contemporary pretrained/transformer baselines and that statistical rigor and some ablation isolation appear incomplete. Given those strengths but important comparative gaps, a middle score reflects fair but not fully convincing comparisons.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper includes reasonable classical baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and Bag-of-Words) and evaluates on multiple datasets with the same metric, but it omits contemporary strong baselines (e.g., transformer fine-tuning or contextual embedding classifiers such as BERT-based models and recent speech-to-text clinical classifiers) that are expected for current work. There is no evidence in the summary of rigorous statistical comparisons or effect-size reporting, and ablations isolating each proposed component (embedding enrichment vs plain co-occurrence vs raw linguistic features) appear incomplete. Overall the comparisons are inadequate for asserting superiority over up-to-date alternatives.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against reasonable, domain-relevant baselines (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and Bag-of-Words) on the same datasets and metric (accuracy), and examines contributions of topology, embeddings, and ensemble/multi-view combinations—so comparisons are largely fair and analyzed. The authors also acknowledge dataset-specific limitations and report modest improvements (e.g., 4.7% on Arizona), indicating awareness when gains are not large. I dock one point because the summary does not mention comparisons to more recent neural/pretrained-text baselines (e.g., transformer-based or other contemporary clinical NLP models), which are relevant to fully establish state-of-the-art performance.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a relevant clinical NLP problem and presents a practical, multilingual evaluation using graph-based features and embeddings, making it appropriate for applied/clinical NLP tracks. Concerns about limited methodological novelty, missing contemporary baselines (e.g., contextual transformers), and incomplete ablation/statistical analysis temper its fit for the very top general ML/NLP venues, so a slightly positive score of 4 reflects solid suitability with room for stronger empirical rigor and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (automated MCI detection from speech transcripts) is relevant to top NLP/ML venues and the paper uses graph-based representations, embeddings, and standard classifiers which are within the community’s interests. However, from the provided summary the empirical evaluation lacks contemporary strong baselines (no transformer fine-tuning or contextual embeddings like BERT), limited ablations isolating each proposed component, and no mention of statistical significance or code release—shortcomings that make its fit to a top venue borderline. The paper does address the right audience and uses reasonable methods, but to be a strong fit it needs more rigorous comparative and statistical evaluation and clearer reproducibility provisions.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a clinically important NLP problem (MCI detection from spontaneous speech) using lightweight, interpretable, and language-independent techniques—word-adjacency networks enriched with word embeddings—evaluated on multiple real datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella Portuguese, Arizona), which aligns well with applied clinical NLP tracks at top NLP/ML venues. The use of multiple baselines (Coh-Metrix, BoW), standard classifiers, multi-view/ensemble strategies, and reported hyperparameters meets the empirical standards I expect. My only concern for a top-tier general NLP/ML venue is that the methodological novelty is modest (graph topological features + embeddings) and the contribution is strongly application-driven; nonetheless, the multilingual evaluation and practical impact make it a good fit.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work sensibly combines known components (word-adjacency networks, embeddings, ensembles) and demonstrates practical gains on short, disfluent clinical transcripts across two languages. While the method is not a fundamentally new modeling paradigm and is largely an engineering integration of existing techniques, the cross-lingual evaluation and targeted adaptation to short clinical transcripts represent a meaningful and useful novelty beyond purely incremental application, meriting a slightly above‑average originality score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines two well-known ingredients—word-adjacency/co-occurrence networks and continuous word embeddings (Bojanowski et al., 2016)—and applies standard topological features plus ensemble/multi-view classifiers to MCI detection. That is a sensible and non-trivial engineering application (notably across short transcripts and two languages), but it is primarily an incremental fusion of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new method or theoretical perspective. The authors do differentiate their approach from prior linguistic and BoW baselines, yet the contribution lacks a novel formal justification or a clearly new modeling paradigm that would surprise an expert.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines word-adjacency/co-occurrence complex network features with continuous word embeddings to better represent short, disfluent clinical transcripts — a practical and concrete extension beyond standard Coh-Metrix and bag-of-words baselines. The cross-lingual evaluation (English and Brazilian Portuguese) and focus on lightweight, topological measures plus ensemble/multi-view combinations differentiate it from prior MCI work that mainly relied on linguistic or BoW features. While the individual components (word embeddings, graph features, ensembles) are known, their integration for short clinical transcripts and demonstrated gains across multiple real datasets is a notable and useful novelty rather than merely incremental.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is adequate across multiple datasets and that the paper demonstrates gains from embedding-enriched complex-network features compared with linguistic and BoW baselines. However, important methodological shortcomings—absence of statistical significance/effect-size reporting and multiple-comparison control, lack of contemporary contextual baselines (e.g., transformer/BERT fine-tuning), limited ablation/sensitivity analyses, and no code/seeds for reproducibility—warrant a moderate score rather than higher.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its embedding-enriched complex-network representation across three relevant datasets and compares against Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia and BoW baselines, with hyperparameters reported and some limitations discussed—so empirical coverage is adequate. However, key theoretical justification and rigorous statistical analysis are missing: no mention of significance testing, effect sizes, or multiple-comparison correction, and contemporary strong baselines (e.g., transformer fine-tuning or contextual BERT features) are absent. Ablation/sensitivity analyses appear incomplete (it is unclear whether each component—embedding enrichment vs plain co-occurrence vs linguistic features—was isolated thoroughly), and reproducibility is limited by no code release and no reporting of seeds/splits despite hyperparameter details. These gaps justify a moderate score.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the experiments are solid and practically oriented: the paper evaluates the method across three real clinical datasets (English and Brazilian Portuguese), compares enriched complex-network features to both linguistic (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia) and BoW baselines, and shows gains from enrichment and multi-view/ensemble combinations. The authors report hyperparameters and discuss important limitations (short transcripts, parsing failures), and they perform sensible comparisons that serve as partial ablations (plain vs enriched networks, feature-type baselines). Missing elements that prevent a top score are lack of explicit code release and somewhat limited discussion of deeper sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of embedding choices or network construction thresholds) and more exhaustive failure-case quantification.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear empirical claims and demonstrates promising results across multiple clinical datasets with reasonable baselines and described methodology. However, they also note important gaps—absence of statistical significance testing/effect sizes, incomplete ablation/isolation of enrichment effects, lack of stronger contemporary baselines and limited reproducibility details—which weaken confidence in the claimed gains. Given the solid empirical work but these methodological and validation shortcomings, a moderate score of 3 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear experimental claims and applies embedding-enriched word-adjacency networks across three datasets with multiple classifiers, which provides some empirical support. However, there is no indication of formal theoretical justification for why embedding enrichment increases discriminative power, ablation completeness is unclear (effect of enrichment vs plain networks vs raw features not fully isolated in the summary), and evaluation lacks mention of statistical significance testing, effect sizes, or contemporary strong baselines (e.g., transformer-based contextual embeddings or recent clinical classifiers). Given these methodological gaps—particularly missing rigorous statistical analysis and stronger baselines—I reserve a moderate score: findings are promising but not yet fully validated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported across multiple real clinical datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella PT, Arizona), with reasonable baselines (Coh-Metrix variants, BoW), reported classifiers/hyperparameters, and practical limitations acknowledged (short transcripts, parsing issues). However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and the claim of language-independence / lightweight deployability would be stronger with more detail on computational costs and replication materials. Overall the experimental methodology appears sound and results justify the conclusions, with only minor gaps in reproducibility and statistical validation.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important clinical-NLP problem and offers a practical, lightweight, multilingual approach that yields consistent empirical gains, making it likely to be adopted in low-resource settings. Although the contribution is not transformative and Reviewer A rightly notes missing comparisons to strong contemporary baselines, ablations/statistics, and released code which limit immediate impact, the demonstrated cross-dataset improvements and deployable design justify a positive score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important clinical-NLP task (MCI detection from speech transcripts) and demonstrates that embedding-enriched co-occurrence networks yield consistent gains across multiple datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, Arizona) and classifiers, suggesting practical value. However, from my perspective the contribution is incremental rather than transformative: claimed improvements (~10% on Cinderella, ~4.7% on Arizona) are promising but the summary lacks evidence of comparison to contemporary strong baselines (e.g., transformer fine-tuning or contextual embeddings) and rigorous statistical analysis/ablation to isolate the embedding-enrichment effect. The method’s language-independence and lightweight nature make it reasonably likely to be reused, but limitations on very short or highly agrammatical transcripts and the absence of reported code reduce immediate community uptake and broader impact.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important clinical task of detecting MCI from speech transcripts and provides a practical, lightweight pipeline that works across English and Brazilian Portuguese — a valuable contribution for low-resource clinical settings. The demonstrated gains from enriching word-adjacency networks with subword-aware embeddings and the empirical improvements (e.g., ~10% on Cinderella and 80% on Arizona with multi-view) suggest a useful alternative to heavy pretrained models and traditional linguistic features. Its emphasis on simple topological features, ensemble/multi-view strategies, and cross-lingual evaluation makes it likely to be adopted or extended by researchers and practitioners working on deployable screening tools. While not transformative, it is a strong, practical advance that opens realistic avenues for low-cost, multilingual MCI screening research and deployment.

main_claims: Complex network features enriched with word embeddings are suitable to detect Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) from speech transcripts and outperform linguistic features in all evaluated datasets.
Enriching word-adjacency/co-occurrence networks with continuous word embeddings (using Bojanowski et al., 2016) improves representation of short transcribed texts for classification.

Combining classifiers via ensemble and multi-view learning further improves detection accuracy (e.g., ~10% improvement on the Cinderella dataset and multi-view produced 80% accuracy on the Arizona dataset, a 4.71% improvement).
The proposed approach is lightweight and language-independent, enabling use on English and Brazilian Portuguese transcripts.
_summary.methodology: Transcripts are mapped to word adjacency/co-occurrence networks, enriched with semantic similarity from word embeddings (trained with Bojanowski et al., 2016), characterized by topological measurements (e.g., PageRank, betweenness, clustering), and used with classifiers (GaussianNB, K-NN, SVM linear/RBF, Random Forest) including ensemble and multi-view combinations to distinguish MCI vs controls.
datasets: Arizona Battery for Communication Disorders of Dementia (ABCD) dataset / Arizona-Battery (Portuguese)
baselines: Linguistic features (Coh-Metrix for English; Coh-Metrix-Dementia for Portuguese)
limitations: MCI disfluencies produce agrammatical speech that impacts parsing results (manual correction not viable for large-scale assessments).
Short transcripts can produce almost-linear networks, making topological measures less informative.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - clinical diagnosis / MCI detection from speech transcripts

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of theoretical work on representation learning and graph-based models.
traits: Penalises empirical papers that lack formal/mathematical justification for the proposed method (e.g., no analysis of why embedding-enriched co-occurrence graphs should improve discriminative power).
Requires statistical rigor: demands per-dataset significance testing with multiple-comparison correction and effect-size reporting; will downgrade if absent or incomplete.

Demands contemporary strong baselines: expects inclusion of at least three recent competitive baselines (e.g., transformer fine-tuning, contextual embedding features like BERT, and recent speech-to-text clinical classifiers); penalises if baselines are stale or missing.

Prefers ablation studies isolating each proposed component (e.g., effect of embedding enrichment vs plain co-occurrence graphs vs raw linguistic features); deducts points if ablations are incomplete or lacking.
Penalises under-specified experimental details that hinder reproducibility: missing hyperparameters, random seeds, training/validation splits, or pre-processing steps will lower the score.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Strictly skeptical of empirical-only claims: I look for formal justification, complete ablations, rigorous statistics, and up-to-date baselines before I accept claimed improvements.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Clinical NLP Practitioner
reviewer_b.background: Research scientist with experience deploying lightweight NLP solutions in low-resource clinical settings and collaborating with neurologists on dementia/MCI screening tools.
traits: Rewards demonstrated practical impact: gives positive weight to methods that improve diagnostic accuracy on real clinical datasets, especially in low-resource or multilingual settings.

Values simplicity, interpretability, and efficiency: prefers lightweight, language-independent approaches that can be deployed without large pretrained transformers; will up-score for methods using shallow classifiers and graph/topological features.

Favors cross-lingual and low-data evaluations: awards points if a paper demonstrates effectiveness on multiple languages (e.g., English and Brazilian Portuguese) or on small/short transcript datasets.
Encourages ensemble and multi-view strategies that yield robust gains across datasets; will give credit when complementary feature views are combined and tested empirically.
Places pragmatic importance on clinical applicability over theoretical novelty: less penalising of omitted theoretical analysis when the empirical results are reproducible and clinically meaningful.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Practicality-first reviewer: I prioritize demonstrated, reproducible improvements on real clinical datasets with lightweight, deployable methods and cross-lingual validation.
